findings.reachability: Any remote attacker who can send HTTP requests can forge all non-connection-level IP headers (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for, cf-connecting-ip, fastly-client-ip, true-client-ip, x-real-ip, x-cluster-client-ip, x-forwarded, forwarded-for, forwarded, x-appengine-user-ip, Cf-Pseudo-IPv4); the library reads them verbatim before falling through to connection.remoteAddress.
findings.justification: Client-supplied headers are prioritised over TCP-level remoteAddress without any trusted-proxy allowlist, so any caller using this IP for access control can be bypassed by setting a spoofed header.

detection_pattern.description: Libraries that return a client IP from user-controlled headers without first verifying the upstream proxy is trusted enable IP-spoofing attacks that bypass IP-based authentication and rate-limiting.

findings.location: is.ip() in lib/is.js line 5, called from getClientIp on every header value
findings.reachability: Any remote attacker who can send a single HTTP request; every header value (x-client-ip, x-forwarded-for entries, etc.) is passed to is.ip() which applies the complex IPv6 regex.
findings.justification: The IPv6 regex contains nested repeating groups (([:A-F]{1,4}:(:| )|){5}) whose alternation creates exponential backtracking paths; a crafted string such as 'A:A:A:A:A:A:A:A:A:!A' can pin the event-loop thread for seconds, constituting a denial-of-service.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: complex IPv6 regex on user input
detection_pattern.description: Hand-rolled IPv6 regexes with nested quantifiers applied to HTTP header values are a well-known ReDoS vector; Node.js's built-in net.isIP() provides a safe, O(n) alternative.

findings.reachability: Any caller that can pass a synthetic or mutated req object where req.raw references back to req (circular) — realistic in test harnesses, proxy middleware chains, or Fastify where req.raw is normally distinct but could be overridden.
findings.justification: The function recurses unconditionally into getClientIp(req.raw) with no depth guard or cycle detection; a circular reference (req.raw = req) causes unlimited recursion and a stack overflow that crashes the Node process.

detection_pattern.description: Request-unwrapping helpers that recurse on req.raw without a depth limit or cycle guard are vulnerable to stack overflow if the wrapped object is ever self-referential.

findings.reachability: Requires an attacker-controlled options object passed to mw(); typical in multi-tenant setups where configuration originates from user data or config files.
findings.justification: configuration.attributeName is used directly as the property name in Object.defineProperty(req, attributeName, ...) without validation; passing '__proto__', 'constructor', or any existing req property name can shadow built-ins or overwrite framework internals on the request object.

detection_pattern.description: Passing an attacker-controlled string as the property name argument to Object.defineProperty can shadow prototype properties or overwrite security-relevant fields on the target object.

findings.reachability: Any request where a header is present but set to null (some HTTP frameworks allow this); the bug is structural and affects every is.ip() call in getClientIp.
findings.justification: Operator precedence means the expression evaluates as (existy(value) && ipv4.test(value)) || ipv6.test(value); the existy guard is not applied to the IPv6 branch, so ipv6.test(null) (which coerces null to the string 'null') runs unconditionally — both inefficient and logically incorrect.

detection_pattern.section_name: Logic bug: missing null guard before second regex branch
detection_pattern.description: When a null-guard is combined with two regex alternatives using && and || without parentheses, operator precedence bypasses the guard for the second alternative, running a potentially complex regex on null/undefined input.
